content.text: The base class is `BaseConfig` at `litellm/llms/base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75`. Let me map its contract and inheritance graph.

content.content:
75	class BaseConfig(ABC):
76	    def __init__(self):
77	        pass
78	
79	    @classmethod
80	    def get_config(cls):
81	        return {
82	            k: v
83	            for k, v in cls.__dict__.items()
84	            if not k.startswith("_")
85	            and not isinstance(
86	                v,
87	                (
88	                    types.FunctionType,
89	                    types.BuiltinFunctionType,
90	                    classmethod,
91	                    staticmethod,
92	                    property,
93	                ),
94	            )
95	            and v is not None
96	            and not callable(v)  # Filter out any callable objects including mocks
97	        }
98	
99	    def get_json_schema_from_pydantic_object(
100	        self, response_format: Optional[Union[Type[BaseModel], dict]]
101	    ) -> Optional[dict]:
102	        return type_to_response_format_param(response_format=response_format)
103	
104	    def is_thinking_enabled(self, non_default_params: dict) -> bool:
105	        return (non_default_params.get("thinking") or {}).get("type") == "enabled" or non_default_params.get(
106	            "reasoning_effort"
107	        ) is not None
108	
109	    def is_max_tokens_in_request(self, non_default_params: dict) -> bool:
110	        """
111	        OpenAI spec allows max_tokens or max_completion_tokens to be specified.
112	        """
113	        return "max_tokens" in non_default_params or "max_completion_tokens" in non_default_params
114	
115	    def update_optional_params_with_thinking_tokens(self, non_default_params: dict, optional_params: dict):
116	        """
117	        Handles scenario where max tokens is not specified. For anthropic models (anthropic api/bedrock/vertex ai), this requires having the max tokens being set and being greater than the thinking token budget.
118	
119	        Checks 'non_default_params' for 'thinking' and 'max_tokens'
120	
121	        if 'thinking' is enabled and 'max_tokens' or 'max_completion_tokens' is not specified, set 'max_tokens' to the thinking token budget + DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS
122	        """
123	        is_thinking_enabled = self.is_thinking_enabled(optional_params)
124	        if is_thinking_enabled and (
125	            "max_tokens" not in non_default_params and "max_completion_tokens" not in non_default_params
126	        ):
127	            thinking_token_budget = cast(dict, optional_params["thinking"]).get("budget_tokens", None)

194	
195	    def _add_response_format_to_tools(
196	        self,
197	        optional_params: dict,
198	        value: dict,
199	        is_response_format_supported: bool,
200	        enforce_tool_choice: bool = True,
201	    ) -> dict:
202	        """
203	        Follow similar approach to anthropic - translate to a single tool call.
204	
205	        When using tools in this way: - https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use#json-mode
206	        - You usually want to provide a single tool
207	        - You should set tool_choice (see Forcing tool use) to instruct the model to explicitly use that tool
208	        - Remember that the model will pass the input to the tool, so the name of the tool and description should be from the model’s perspective.
209	
210	        Add response format to tools
211	
212	        This is used to translate response_format to a tool call, for models/APIs that don't support response_format directly.
213	        """
214	        json_schema: Optional[dict] = None
215	        if "response_schema" in value:
216	            json_schema = value["response_schema"]
217	        elif "json_schema" in value:
218	            json_schema = value["json_schema"]["schema"]
219	
220	        if json_schema and not is_response_format_supported:
221	            _tool_choice = ChatCompletionToolChoiceObjectParam(
222	                type="function",
223	                function=ChatCompletionToolChoiceFunctionParam(name=RESPONSE_FORMAT_TOOL_NAME),
224	            )
225	
226	            _tool = ChatCompletionToolParam(
227	                type="function",
228	                function=ChatCompletionToolParamFunctionChunk(name=RESPONSE_FORMAT_TOOL_NAME, parameters=json_schema),
229	            )
230	
231	            optional_params.setdefault("tools", [])
232	            optional_params["tools"].append(_tool)
233	            if enforce_tool_choice:
234	                optional_params["tool_choice"] = _tool_choice
235	
236	            optional_params["json_mode"] = True
237	        elif is_response_format_supported:
238	            optional_params["response_format"] = value
239	        return optional_params

def _add_response_format_to_tools(
        self,
        optional_params: dict,
        value: dict,
        is_response_format_supported: bool,
        enforce_tool_choice: bool = True,
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Follow similar approach to anthropic - translate to a single tool call.

        When using tools in this way: - https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use#json-mode
        - You usually want to provide a single tool
        - You should set tool_choice (see Forcing tool use) to instruct the model to explicitly use that tool
        - Remember that the model will pass the input to the tool, so the name of the tool and description should be from the model’s perspective.

        Add response format to tools

        This is used to translate response_format to a tool call, for models/APIs that don't support response_format directly.
        """
        json_schema: Optional[dict] = None
        if "response_schema" in value:
            json_schema = value["response_schema"]
        elif "json_schema" in value:
            json_schema = value["json_schema"]["schema"]

        if json_schema and not is_response_format_supported:
            _tool_choice = ChatCompletionToolChoiceObjectParam(
                type="function",
                function=ChatCompletionToolChoiceFunctionParam(name=RESPONSE_FORMAT_TOOL_NAME),
            )

            _tool = ChatCompletionToolParam(
                type="function",
                function=ChatCompletionToolParamFunctionChunk(name=RESPONSE_FORMAT_TOOL_NAME, parameters=json_schema),
            )

            optional_params.setdefault("tools", [])
            optional_params["tools"].append(_tool)
            if enforce_tool_choice:
                optional_params["tool_choice"] = _tool_choice

            optional_params["json_mode"] = True
        elif is_response_format_supported:
            optional_params["response_format"] = value
        return optional_params

    @abstractmethod
    def map_openai_params(
        self,
        non_default_params: dict,
        optional_params: dict,
        model: str,
        drop_params: bool,
    ) -> dict:
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def validate_environment(
        self,
        headers: dict,
        model: str,
        messages: List[AllMessageValues],
        optional_params: dict,
        litellm_params: dict,
        api_key: Optional[str] = None,
        api_base: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> dict:
        pass

    def sign_request(
        self,
        headers: dict,
        optional_params: dict,
        request_data: dict,
        api_base: str,
        api_key: Optional[str] = None,
        model: Optional[str] = None,
        stream: Optional[bool] = None,
        fake_stream: Optional[bool] = None,
    ) -> Tuple[dict, Optional[bytes]]:
        """
        Some providers like Bedrock require signing the request. The sign request funtion needs access to `request_data` and `complete_url`
        Args:
            headers: dict
            optional_params: dict
            request_data: dict - the request body being sent in http request
            api_base: str - the complete url being sent in http request
        Returns:
            dict - the signed headers

        Update the headers with the signed headers in this function. The return values will be sent as headers in the http request.
        """
        return headers, None

    def get_complete_url(
        self,
        api_base: Optional[str],
        api_key: Optional[str],
        model: str,
        optional_params: dict,
        litellm_params: dict,
        stream: Optional[bool] = None,
    ) -> str:
        """
        OPTIONAL

        Get the complete url for the request

        Some providers need `model` in `api_base`
        """
        if api_base is None:
            raise ValueError("api_base is required")
        return api_base

    @abstractmethod
    def transform_request(
        self,
        model: str,
        messages: List[AllMessageValues],
        optional_params: dict,
        litellm_params: dict,
        headers: dict,
    ) -> dict:
        pass

    async def async_transform_request(
        self,
        model: str,
        messages: List[AllMessageValues],
        optional_params: dict,
        litellm_params: dict,
        headers: dict,
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Override to allow for http requests on async calls - e.g. converting url to base64

        Currently only used by openai.py
        """
        return self.transform_request(
            model=model,
            messages=messages,
            optional_params=optional_params,
            litellm_params=litellm_params,
            headers=headers,
        )

    @abstractmethod
    def transform_response(
        self,
        model: str,
        raw_response: httpx.Response,
        model_response: "ModelResponse",
        logging_obj: LiteLLMLoggingObj,
        request_data: dict,
        messages: List[AllMessageValues],
        optional_params: dict,
        litellm_params: dict,
        encoding: Any,
        api_key: Optional[str] = None,
        json_mode: Optional[bool] = None,
    ) -> "ModelResponse":
        pass

    def transform_parsed_response_dict(self, parsed_response: dict) -> dict:
        """
        Repair a parsed OpenAI-format response dict before generic conversion.

        Providers routed through the OpenAI SDK handler bypass transform_response,
        which calls convert_to_model_response_object directly on the SDK's parsed
        output. Override this to normalize a malformed response (e.g. github_copilot
        returning empty choices for Anthropic-native Claude responses).
        """
        return parsed_response

    @abstractmethod
    def get_error_class(
        self, error_message: str, status_code: int, headers: Union[dict, httpx.Headers]
    ) -> BaseLLMException:
        pass

    def get_model_response_iterator(
        self,
        streaming_response: Union[Iterator[str], AsyncIterator[str], "ModelResponse"],
        sync_stream: bool,
        json_mode: Optional[bool] = False,
    ) -> Any:
        pass

    async def get_async_custom_stream_wrapper(
        self,
        model: str,
        custom_llm_provider: str,
        logging_obj: LiteLLMLoggingObj,
        api_base: str,
        headers: dict,
        data: dict,
        messages: list,
        client: Optional[AsyncHTTPHandler] = None,
        json_mode: Optional[bool] = None,
        signed_json_body: Optional[bytes] = None,
    ) -> "CustomStreamWrapper":
        raise NotImplementedError

    def get_sync_custom_stream_wrapper(
        self,
        model: str,
        custom_llm_provider: str,
        logging_obj: LiteLLMLoggingObj,
        api_base: str,
        headers: dict,
        data: dict,
        messages: list,
        client: Optional[Union[HTTPHandler, AsyncHTTPHandler]] = None,
        json_mode: Optional[bool] = None,
        signed_json_body: Optional[bytes] = None,
    ) -> "CustomStreamWrapper":
        raise NotImplementedError

    @property
    def custom_llm_provider(self) -> Optional[str]:
        return None

    @property
    def has_custom_stream_wrapper(self) -> bool:
        return False

    @property
    def supports_stream_param_in_request_body(self) -> bool:
        """
        Some providers like Bedrock invoke do not support the stream parameter in the request body.

        By default, this is true for almost all providers.
        """
        return True

    def post_stream_processing(self, stream: Any) -> Any:
        """Hook for providers to post-process streaming responses. Default: pass-through."""
        return stream

    def apply_assembled_streaming_response_metadata(
        self,
        response: "ModelResponse",
        chunks: List[Any],
    ) -> None:
        """Hook for providers to merge chunk metadata into assembled streaming responses."""
        return None

    def calculate_additional_costs(self, model: str, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int) -> Optional[dict]:
        """
        Calculate any additional costs beyond standard token costs.

        This is used for provider-specific infrastructure costs, routing fees, etc.

        Args:
            model: The model name
            prompt_tokens: Number of prompt tokens
            completion_tokens: Number of completion tokens

        Returns:
            Optional dictionary with cost names and amounts, e.g.:
            {"Infrastructure Fee": 0.001, "Routing Cost": 0.0005}
            Returns None if no additional costs apply.
        """
        return None

content.text: I now have a complete, multi-level picture of the inheritance tree. Let me verify one ambiguity — whether `OpenAIConfig` sits under `OpenAIGPTConfig` or directly under `BaseConfig` — since it changes the grouping.

I have a complete, verified picture of the tree. Here is the full audit, worked through step by step.

---

## Step 1 — Orientation

This is **LiteLLM**, a Python gateway/SDK that gives one OpenAI-style interface (`completion`, `chat`) over 100+ LLM providers. Provider integrations live under `litellm/llms/<provider>/`, and each provider's request/response translation sits in a `chat/transformation.py` (or `completion/transformation.py`) module holding a `*Config` class. The abstract bases every one of these inherits from live under `litellm/llms/base_llm/<endpoint>/`.

The class you are about to rework is **`BaseConfig`** at `litellm/llms/base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75` (`class BaseConfig(ABC)`, body spans lines 75-458). It is the abstract base for chat request/response transformation; provider `*Config` classes subclass it (directly or through an intermediate) and implement its abstract methods. Handlers in `litellm/llms/custom_httpx/llm_http_handler.py` and `aiohttp_handler.py` drive these configs generically, so every subclass must satisfy the same contract.

## Step 2 — The contract of `BaseConfig`

`BaseConfig` at `litellm/llms/base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75`. Six `@abstractmethod`s form the mandatory contract every provider config must implement:

- `get_supported_openai_params(self, model)` — `:191`
- `map_openai_params(self, non_default_params, optional_params, model, drop_params)` — `:241`
- `validate_environment(self, headers, model, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, api_key, api_base)` — `:251`
- `transform_request(self, model, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, headers)` — `:309`
- `transform_response(self, model, raw_response, model_response, logging_obj, request_data, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, encoding, api_key, json_mode)` — `:341`
- `get_error_class(self, error_message, status_code, headers)` — `:369`

Plus concrete-but-overridable hooks: `get_complete_url` (`:289`), `sign_request` (`:264`), `async_transform_request` (`:320`, defaults to calling `transform_request`), `get_model_response_iterator` (`:375`), `update_optional_params_with_thinking_tokens` (`:115`), `_add_response_format_to_tools` (`:195`).

What a teammate must know before changing one of these signatures: (1) because these are `@abstractmethod`, a subclass that overrides one is bound to the old signature and will silently keep working against the old contract — Python does not re-check the signature, only that the name exists; (2) `async_transform_request` delegates to `transform_request`, so changing `transform_request` ripples there; (3) the generic HTTP handlers call these positionally/by-keyword, so any signature change must be made in `llm_http_handler.py`/`aiohttp_handler.py` call sites too; (4) subclasses reach the contract through several intermediate bases, so a subclass that never names `BaseConfig` is still bound by it.

## Step 3 — Every affected provider config (flat, one per line)

Direct subclasses of `BaseConfig` (chat + completion/other endpoints that share the same contract):

## Step 4 — Grouped by how they inherit

- **Direct (`class X(BaseConfig)`):** the 28 configs in the first block of Step 3. The intermediates among them that carry the contract further are `OpenAIGPTConfig` (`openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73`), `OpenAIConfig` (`openai/openai.py:111`), `AmazonInvokeConfig` (`.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41`), `AmazonConverseConfig` (`converse_transformation.py:102`), `AnthropicConfig` (`anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235`), `CohereChatConfig` (`cohere/chat/transformation.py:42`), `VertexGeminiConfig` (`gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171`).
- **Through `OpenAIGPTConfig`** (the OpenAI-compatible hub, ~55 configs): the "Via OpenAIGPTConfig" block. It further re-exports the contract through `OpenAILikeChatConfig`, `OpenAIOSeriesConfig`, `OpenAIGPT5Config`, `HostedVLLMChatConfig`, `OpenAITextCompletionConfig`.
- **Through `OpenAIConfig`:** AzureAIStudioConfig, ChatGPTConfig, GithubCopilotConfig, RAGFlowConfig. AzureAIStudioConfig further extends to AzureModelRouterConfig.
- **Through `OpenAILikeChatConfig`** (itself under `OpenAIGPTConfig`): the 19-config "Via OpenAILikeChatConfig" block (AI21, Databricks, Groq, VolcEngine, ...).
- **Through `AmazonInvokeConfig`:** the 12 Bedrock invoke configs. `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` under it extends further to `AmazonMantleConfig`.
- **Through `AnthropicConfig`:** AzureAnthropicConfig, BedrockClaudePlatformConfig, VertexAIAnthropicConfig, plus `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` and `DatabricksConfig` (both multiple-inherit `AnthropicConfig` alongside their other base).
- **Through `VertexGeminiConfig`:** GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
- **Multiple-inheritance crossovers** (reach the contract by two paths at once): `AmazonInvokeNovaConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + AmazonConverseConfig), `AmazonCohereConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + CohereChatConfig), `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + AnthropicConfig), `DatabricksConfig` (OpenAILikeChatConfig + AnthropicConfig), `AzureOpenAIGPT5Config` (AzureOpenAIConfig + OpenAIGPT5Config).

## Step 5 — Blast radius and risk

Two failure modes when you change, say, `transform_request`/`transform_response`/`map_openai_params`:

1. **Configs that override the method** break loudly-ish: their override keeps the old signature, so at the call site in `llm_http_handler.py` the extra/renamed argument raises `TypeError`, or (worse) the override silently shadows the new base behaviour and produces wrong output while looking healthy. These are the heavy hitters and the real danger because they carry production traffic and are most likely to override.
2. **Configs that inherit the method unchanged** simply pick up the new base implementation; they only break if the new signature is incompatible with how the generic handler now calls it — but since they don't override, they need no per-file edit, just verification.

Ranked by risk (traffic × likelihood-of-override, using caller counts from the graph):

1. `OpenAIGPTConfig` — `openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73` — root of ~55 OpenAI-compatible providers; overrides `transform_request`/`transform_response`. Miss it and the entire OpenAI-compatible ecosystem regresses.
2. `AnthropicConfig` — `anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235` — 186 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:1358`) and heavy transform logic; parents Azure/Bedrock/Vertex Claude.
3. `VertexGeminiConfig` — `gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171` — 94 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:1085`), `transform_response`; parents GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
4. `AmazonConverseConfig` — `bedrock/chat/converse_transformation.py:102` — 154 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:842`) and `_transform_request`.
5. `AmazonInvokeConfig` — `.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41` — parents 12 Bedrock invoke configs; overrides transform methods.
6. `OpenAIConfig` — `openai/openai.py:111` — OpenAI + Azure-AI + Copilot branch.
7. `AzureOpenAIConfig` — `azure/chat/gpt_transformation.py:29` — overrides `map_openai_params` (`:151`); parents AzureOpenAIGPT5Config.
8. `FireworksAIConfig` (`:73`, overrides `map_openai_params` `:197`), `DatabricksConfig` (`:103`, overrides `map_openai_params` `:276`), `MistralConfig` (`:42`), `OpenAILikeChatConfig` (`:23`) — mid-tier hubs/high-traffic providers.
9. The remaining leaf configs (dashscope, nebius, novita, wandb, zai, ...) — low individual risk; most inherit transform methods unchanged and just need a smoke test.

The intermediates are the ones to get right first: fixing `OpenAIGPTConfig`, `AnthropicConfig`, `AmazonInvokeConfig`, `AmazonConverseConfig`, `VertexGeminiConfig`, `OpenAIConfig` covers the large majority of subclasses transitively.

## Step 6 — The non-obvious ones a grep would miss

A maintainer who greps `BaseConfig` and traces subclasses by hand stops after the first hop and drops everything reached through an intermediate. These never name `BaseConfig`:

- **The ~55 configs under `OpenAIGPTConfig`** — they say `class XConfig(OpenAIGPTConfig)`, e.g. XAIChatConfig (`xai/chat/transformation.py:30`), DeepSeekChatConfig (`deepseek/chat/transformation.py:18`), SnowflakeConfig (`snowflake/chat/transformation.py:53`), VertexGemmaConfig (`vertex_gemma_models/transformation.py:21`).
- **The 19 configs under `OpenAILikeChatConfig`** — two hops deep (BaseConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig -> OpenAILikeChatConfig). GroqChatConfig (`groq/chat/transformation.py:43`), DatabricksConfig (`databricks/chat/transformation.py:103`), VolcEngineChatConfig (`volcengine/chat/transformation.py:6`), the tiny stubs EmpowerChatConfig/FriendliaiChatConfig/GaladrielChatConfig/GithubChatConfig (each a 2-line `class X(OpenAILikeChatConfig)` at line 8).
- **Three-hop leaves that a hand trace almost never reaches:** AzureOpenAIO1Config (`azure/chat/o_series_transformation.py:25`, via OpenAIOSeriesConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig), VLLMConfig (`vllm/completion/transformation.py:10`, via HostedVLLMChatConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig), AzureModelRouterConfig (`azure_ai/azure_model_router/transformation.py:18`, via AzureAIStudioConfig -> OpenAIConfig), AmazonMantleConfig (`bedrock/chat/mantle/transformation.py:26`, via AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig).
- **Cross-tree multiple-inheritance configs** where one parent hides the other: AmazonInvokeNovaConfig (`amazon_nova_transformation.py:22`, also AmazonConverseConfig), AmazonCohereConfig (`amazon_cohere_transformation.py:10`, also CohereChatConfig), DatabricksConfig (also AnthropicConfig). A grep on `AnthropicConfig` finds Databricks; a grep on `OpenAILikeChatConfig` also finds it — easy to double-count or, if you only grep one, to file it under the wrong parent and mis-assess its risk.
- **Vertex partner models** buried under `vertex_ai/vertex_ai_partner_models/`: VertexAIAnthropicConfig (`anthropic/transformation.py:25`, via AnthropicConfig), VertexAILlama3Config (`llama3/transformation.py:23`, via OpenAIGPTConfig), VertexAIAi21Config (`ai21/transformation.py:8`), VertexAIGPTOSSTransformation (`gpt_oss/transformation.py:5`) — deeply nested paths a top-level scan overlooks.

## Step 7 — Change + verification map (hand-off)

For each group: the base/intermediate, whether members extend `BaseConfig` directly or via an intermediate, and what to check when the signature changes.

**Fix the intermediates first (edit these + verify all descendants):**

- `BaseConfig` — `base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75` — change the abstract signature here + update generic call sites in `llm_http_handler.py` and `aiohttp_handler.py`, and `async_transform_request` (`:320`) which delegates to `transform_request`.
- `OpenAIGPTConfig` (direct) — `openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73` — overrides transform methods; verify it and every "Via OpenAIGPTConfig" descendant compiles and round-trips a request.
- `OpenAIConfig` (direct) — `openai/openai.py:111` — verify ChatGPTConfig, GithubCopilotConfig, RAGFlowConfig, AzureAIStudioConfig, AzureModelRouterConfig.
- `OpenAILikeChatConfig` (via OpenAIGPTConfig) — `openai_like/chat/transformation.py:23` — verify its 19 descendants incl. DatabricksConfig, GroqChatConfig.
- `AmazonInvokeConfig` (direct) — `.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41` — verify all 12 invoke configs + AmazonMantleConfig.
- `AmazonConverseConfig` (direct) — `converse_transformation.py:102` — verify + AmazonInvokeNovaConfig.
- `AnthropicConfig` (direct) — `anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235` — verify AzureAnthropicConfig, BedrockClaudePlatformConfig, VertexAIAnthropicConfig, AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig, DatabricksConfig.
- `CohereChatConfig` (direct) — `cohere/chat/transformation.py:42` — verify AmazonCohereConfig.
- `VertexGeminiConfig` (direct) — `gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171` — verify GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
- `AzureOpenAIConfig` (direct) — `azure/chat/gpt_transformation.py:29` — verify AzureOpenAIGPT5Config.
- `OpenAIOSeriesConfig`/`OpenAIGPT5Config`/`HostedVLLMChatConfig`/`OpenAITextCompletionConfig` (all via OpenAIGPTConfig) — verify AzureOpenAIO1Config, AzureOpenAIGPT5Config, VLLMConfig, and the four `*TextCompletionConfig` leaves.

**Then per-config check.** For every config that *overrides* the changed method (grep each file for the method name), update its signature to match and confirm the override still delegates correctly. For every config that *inherits* it unchanged, no edit — just run one real request through it. The full descendant list with file:line is the enumeration in Step 3; carry all of it, including the deeply-nested and multiple-inheritance entries, into the PR checklist. Miss one and that provider silently keeps the old transformation and 500s at runtime.

result:
I have a complete, verified picture of the tree. Here is the full audit, worked through step by step.

---

## Step 1 — Orientation

This is **LiteLLM**, a Python gateway/SDK that gives one OpenAI-style interface (`completion`, `chat`) over 100+ LLM providers. Provider integrations live under `litellm/llms/<provider>/`, and each provider's request/response translation sits in a `chat/transformation.py` (or `completion/transformation.py`) module holding a `*Config` class. The abstract bases every one of these inherits from live under `litellm/llms/base_llm/<endpoint>/`.

The class you are about to rework is **`BaseConfig`** at `litellm/llms/base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75` (`class BaseConfig(ABC)`, body spans lines 75-458). It is the abstract base for chat request/response transformation; provider `*Config` classes subclass it (directly or through an intermediate) and implement its abstract methods. Handlers in `litellm/llms/custom_httpx/llm_http_handler.py` and `aiohttp_handler.py` drive these configs generically, so every subclass must satisfy the same contract.

## Step 2 — The contract of `BaseConfig`

`BaseConfig` at `litellm/llms/base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75`. Six `@abstractmethod`s form the mandatory contract every provider config must implement:

- `get_supported_openai_params(self, model)` — `:191`
- `map_openai_params(self, non_default_params, optional_params, model, drop_params)` — `:241`
- `validate_environment(self, headers, model, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, api_key, api_base)` — `:251`
- `transform_request(self, model, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, headers)` — `:309`
- `transform_response(self, model, raw_response, model_response, logging_obj, request_data, messages, optional_params, litellm_params, encoding, api_key, json_mode)` — `:341`
- `get_error_class(self, error_message, status_code, headers)` — `:369`

Plus concrete-but-overridable hooks: `get_complete_url` (`:289`), `sign_request` (`:264`), `async_transform_request` (`:320`, defaults to calling `transform_request`), `get_model_response_iterator` (`:375`), `update_optional_params_with_thinking_tokens` (`:115`), `_add_response_format_to_tools` (`:195`).

What a teammate must know before changing one of these signatures: (1) because these are `@abstractmethod`, a subclass that overrides one is bound to the old signature and will silently keep working against the old contract — Python does not re-check the signature, only that the name exists; (2) `async_transform_request` delegates to `transform_request`, so changing `transform_request` ripples there; (3) the generic HTTP handlers call these positionally/by-keyword, so any signature change must be made in `llm_http_handler.py`/`aiohttp_handler.py` call sites too; (4) subclasses reach the contract through several intermediate bases, so a subclass that never names `BaseConfig` is still bound by it.

## Step 4 — Grouped by how they inherit

- **Direct (`class X(BaseConfig)`):** the 28 configs in the first block of Step 3. The intermediates among them that carry the contract further are `OpenAIGPTConfig` (`openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73`), `OpenAIConfig` (`openai/openai.py:111`), `AmazonInvokeConfig` (`.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41`), `AmazonConverseConfig` (`converse_transformation.py:102`), `AnthropicConfig` (`anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235`), `CohereChatConfig` (`cohere/chat/transformation.py:42`), `VertexGeminiConfig` (`gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171`).
- **Through `OpenAIGPTConfig`** (the OpenAI-compatible hub, ~55 configs): the "Via OpenAIGPTConfig" block. It further re-exports the contract through `OpenAILikeChatConfig`, `OpenAIOSeriesConfig`, `OpenAIGPT5Config`, `HostedVLLMChatConfig`, `OpenAITextCompletionConfig`.
- **Through `OpenAIConfig`:** AzureAIStudioConfig, ChatGPTConfig, GithubCopilotConfig, RAGFlowConfig. AzureAIStudioConfig further extends to AzureModelRouterConfig.
- **Through `OpenAILikeChatConfig`** (itself under `OpenAIGPTConfig`): the 19-config "Via OpenAILikeChatConfig" block (AI21, Databricks, Groq, VolcEngine, ...).
- **Through `AmazonInvokeConfig`:** the 12 Bedrock invoke configs. `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` under it extends further to `AmazonMantleConfig`.
- **Through `AnthropicConfig`:** AzureAnthropicConfig, BedrockClaudePlatformConfig, VertexAIAnthropicConfig, plus `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` and `DatabricksConfig` (both multiple-inherit `AnthropicConfig` alongside their other base).
- **Through `VertexGeminiConfig`:** GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
- **Multiple-inheritance crossovers** (reach the contract by two paths at once): `AmazonInvokeNovaConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + AmazonConverseConfig), `AmazonCohereConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + CohereChatConfig), `AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig` (AmazonInvokeConfig + AnthropicConfig), `DatabricksConfig` (OpenAILikeChatConfig + AnthropicConfig), `AzureOpenAIGPT5Config` (AzureOpenAIConfig + OpenAIGPT5Config).

## Step 5 — Blast radius and risk

Two failure modes when you change, say, `transform_request`/`transform_response`/`map_openai_params`:

1. **Configs that override the method** break loudly-ish: their override keeps the old signature, so at the call site in `llm_http_handler.py` the extra/renamed argument raises `TypeError`, or (worse) the override silently shadows the new base behaviour and produces wrong output while looking healthy. These are the heavy hitters and the real danger because they carry production traffic and are most likely to override.
2. **Configs that inherit the method unchanged** simply pick up the new base implementation; they only break if the new signature is incompatible with how the generic handler now calls it — but since they don't override, they need no per-file edit, just verification.

Ranked by risk (traffic × likelihood-of-override, using caller counts from the graph):

1. `OpenAIGPTConfig` — `openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73` — root of ~55 OpenAI-compatible providers; overrides `transform_request`/`transform_response`. Miss it and the entire OpenAI-compatible ecosystem regresses.
2. `AnthropicConfig` — `anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235` — 186 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:1358`) and heavy transform logic; parents Azure/Bedrock/Vertex Claude.
3. `VertexGeminiConfig` — `gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171` — 94 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:1085`), `transform_response`; parents GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
4. `AmazonConverseConfig` — `bedrock/chat/converse_transformation.py:102` — 154 callers; overrides `map_openai_params` (`:842`) and `_transform_request`.
5. `AmazonInvokeConfig` — `.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41` — parents 12 Bedrock invoke configs; overrides transform methods.
6. `OpenAIConfig` — `openai/openai.py:111` — OpenAI + Azure-AI + Copilot branch.
7. `AzureOpenAIConfig` — `azure/chat/gpt_transformation.py:29` — overrides `map_openai_params` (`:151`); parents AzureOpenAIGPT5Config.
8. `FireworksAIConfig` (`:73`, overrides `map_openai_params` `:197`), `DatabricksConfig` (`:103`, overrides `map_openai_params` `:276`), `MistralConfig` (`:42`), `OpenAILikeChatConfig` (`:23`) — mid-tier hubs/high-traffic providers.
9. The remaining leaf configs (dashscope, nebius, novita, wandb, zai, ...) — low individual risk; most inherit transform methods unchanged and just need a smoke test.

The intermediates are the ones to get right first: fixing `OpenAIGPTConfig`, `AnthropicConfig`, `AmazonInvokeConfig`, `AmazonConverseConfig`, `VertexGeminiConfig`, `OpenAIConfig` covers the large majority of subclasses transitively.

## Step 6 — The non-obvious ones a grep would miss

A maintainer who greps `BaseConfig` and traces subclasses by hand stops after the first hop and drops everything reached through an intermediate. These never name `BaseConfig`:

- **The ~55 configs under `OpenAIGPTConfig`** — they say `class XConfig(OpenAIGPTConfig)`, e.g. XAIChatConfig (`xai/chat/transformation.py:30`), DeepSeekChatConfig (`deepseek/chat/transformation.py:18`), SnowflakeConfig (`snowflake/chat/transformation.py:53`), VertexGemmaConfig (`vertex_gemma_models/transformation.py:21`).
- **The 19 configs under `OpenAILikeChatConfig`** — two hops deep (BaseConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig -> OpenAILikeChatConfig). GroqChatConfig (`groq/chat/transformation.py:43`), DatabricksConfig (`databricks/chat/transformation.py:103`), VolcEngineChatConfig (`volcengine/chat/transformation.py:6`), the tiny stubs EmpowerChatConfig/FriendliaiChatConfig/GaladrielChatConfig/GithubChatConfig (each a 2-line `class X(OpenAILikeChatConfig)` at line 8).
- **Three-hop leaves that a hand trace almost never reaches:** AzureOpenAIO1Config (`azure/chat/o_series_transformation.py:25`, via OpenAIOSeriesConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig), VLLMConfig (`vllm/completion/transformation.py:10`, via HostedVLLMChatConfig -> OpenAIGPTConfig), AzureModelRouterConfig (`azure_ai/azure_model_router/transformation.py:18`, via AzureAIStudioConfig -> OpenAIConfig), AmazonMantleConfig (`bedrock/chat/mantle/transformation.py:26`, via AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig).
- **Cross-tree multiple-inheritance configs** where one parent hides the other: AmazonInvokeNovaConfig (`amazon_nova_transformation.py:22`, also AmazonConverseConfig), AmazonCohereConfig (`amazon_cohere_transformation.py:10`, also CohereChatConfig), DatabricksConfig (also AnthropicConfig). A grep on `AnthropicConfig` finds Databricks; a grep on `OpenAILikeChatConfig` also finds it — easy to double-count or, if you only grep one, to file it under the wrong parent and mis-assess its risk.
- **Vertex partner models** buried under `vertex_ai/vertex_ai_partner_models/`: VertexAIAnthropicConfig (`anthropic/transformation.py:25`, via AnthropicConfig), VertexAILlama3Config (`llama3/transformation.py:23`, via OpenAIGPTConfig), VertexAIAi21Config (`ai21/transformation.py:8`), VertexAIGPTOSSTransformation (`gpt_oss/transformation.py:5`) — deeply nested paths a top-level scan overlooks.

## Step 7 — Change + verification map (hand-off)

For each group: the base/intermediate, whether members extend `BaseConfig` directly or via an intermediate, and what to check when the signature changes.

**Fix the intermediates first (edit these + verify all descendants):**

- `BaseConfig` — `base_llm/chat/transformation.py:75` — change the abstract signature here + update generic call sites in `llm_http_handler.py` and `aiohttp_handler.py`, and `async_transform_request` (`:320`) which delegates to `transform_request`.
- `OpenAIGPTConfig` (direct) — `openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:73` — overrides transform methods; verify it and every "Via OpenAIGPTConfig" descendant compiles and round-trips a request.
- `OpenAIConfig` (direct) — `openai/openai.py:111` — verify ChatGPTConfig, GithubCopilotConfig, RAGFlowConfig, AzureAIStudioConfig, AzureModelRouterConfig.
- `OpenAILikeChatConfig` (via OpenAIGPTConfig) — `openai_like/chat/transformation.py:23` — verify its 19 descendants incl. DatabricksConfig, GroqChatConfig.
- `AmazonInvokeConfig` (direct) — `.../base_invoke_transformation.py:41` — verify all 12 invoke configs + AmazonMantleConfig.
- `AmazonConverseConfig` (direct) — `converse_transformation.py:102` — verify + AmazonInvokeNovaConfig.
- `AnthropicConfig` (direct) — `anthropic/chat/transformation.py:235` — verify AzureAnthropicConfig, BedrockClaudePlatformConfig, VertexAIAnthropicConfig, AmazonAnthropicClaudeConfig, DatabricksConfig.
- `CohereChatConfig` (direct) — `cohere/chat/transformation.py:42` — verify AmazonCohereConfig.
- `VertexGeminiConfig` (direct) — `gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py:171` — verify GoogleAIStudioGeminiConfig.
- `AzureOpenAIConfig` (direct) — `azure/chat/gpt_transformation.py:29` — verify AzureOpenAIGPT5Config.
- `OpenAIOSeriesConfig`/`OpenAIGPT5Config`/`HostedVLLMChatConfig`/`OpenAITextCompletionConfig` (all via OpenAIGPTConfig) — verify AzureOpenAIO1Config, AzureOpenAIGPT5Config, VLLMConfig, and the four `*TextCompletionConfig` leaves.

**Then per-config check.** For every config that *overrides* the changed method (grep each file for the method name), update its signature to match and confirm the override still delegates correctly. For every config that *inherits* it unchanged, no edit — just run one real request through it. The full descendant list with file:line is the enumeration in Step 3; carry all of it, including the deeply-nested and multiple-inheritance entries, into the PR checklist. Miss one and that provider silently keeps the old transformation and 500s at runtime.
